title: Apportion Seats
text: apportion provides different apportionment methods allocating seats across states. methods also sometimes used allocating seats votes proportional representation systems.

title: Apportion Seats
text: can install development version apportion like :

title: Apportion Seats
text: apportion several apportionment methods, take two required arguments: - size: number seats apportion across units - pop: vector population sizes unit Implemented methods include: Adams Method (app_adams()) Balinski Young Method (app_balinski_young()) Dean Method (app_dean()) D’Hondt Method (app_dhondt()) Hamilton-Vinton Method (app_hamilton_vinton()) Huntington-Hill Method (app_huntington_hill()) Jefferson Method (app_jefferson()) Webster Method (app_webster())

what: Apportion by the Adams Method — app_adams
title: Apportion by the Adams Method — app_adams
text: divisor method uses ceiling (round-) rounding, proposed John Quincy Adams. never adopted US Congressional apportionment.

title: Apportion by the Adams Method — app_adams
Apportion by the Adams Method — app_adams
text: size integer number seats apportion across units pop vector population sizes unit

title: Apportion by the Adams Method — app_adams
text: integer vector length pop number seats apportioned unit.

title: Apportion by the Adams Method — app_adams
text: Adams method finds common divisor \(d\) ceiling-rounded quotients sum desired house size: $$\sum_{} \left\lceil \frac{p_i}{d} \right\rceil = H$$ \(p_i\) population unit \(\) \(H\) total number seats (size). divisor \(d\) adjusted iteratively condition met. every non-zero fractional remainder always rounded , Adams method generous small units among classical divisor methods. unit positive population receives least one seat, can -represent small states parties relative population share.

title: Apportion by the Adams Method — app_adams

what: Apportion by the Balinski-Young Method — app_balinski_young
title: Apportion by the Balinski-Young Method — app_balinski_young
text: quota method satisfies quota property avoids Alabama paradox, proposed Michel Balinski H. Peyton Young.

title: Apportion by the Balinski-Young Method — app_balinski_young
Apportion by the Balinski-Young Method — app_balinski_young
text: size integer number seats apportion across units pop vector population sizes unit

title: Apportion by the Balinski-Young Method — app_balinski_young
text: integer vector length pop number seats apportioned unit.

title: Apportion by the Balinski-Young Method — app_balinski_young
text: Let exact quota unit \(\) : $$q_i = \frac{p_i \cdot H}{P}$$ \(p_i\) population unit \(\), \(H\) total number seats (size), \(P = \sum_i p_i\) total population. method guarantees quota property: unit receives either \(\lfloor q_i \rfloor\) \(\lceil q_i \rceil\) seats, unit - -represented one seat relative exact quota. Seats awarded sequentially using D'Hondt priority \(p_i / (1 + n_i)\), upper quota cap: unit received \(\lceil q_i \rceil\) seats excluded consideration. cap prevents runaway -representation unconstrained Jefferson/D'Hondt method can produce, preserving freedom "Alabama paradox," increasing total house size can paradoxically cause unit lose seat. Balinski Young proved apportionment method can simultaneously satisfy quota property avoid paradoxes; method practical compromise prioritizes quota property.

title: Apportion by the Balinski-Young Method — app_balinski_young
text: Balinski, M. L., & Young, H. P. (2001). Fair Representation: Meeting Ideal One Man, One Vote (2nd ed.). Brookings Institution Press.

title: Apportion by the Balinski-Young Method — app_balinski_young

what: Apportion by the Dean Method — app_dean
title: Apportion by the Dean Method — app_dean
text: divisor method rounds harmonic mean consecutive integers, proposed astronomer James Dean. never adopted US Congressional apportionment.

title: Apportion by the Dean Method — app_dean
Apportion by the Dean Method — app_dean
text: size integer number seats apportion across units pop vector population sizes unit

title: Apportion by the Dean Method — app_dean
text: integer vector length pop number seats apportioned unit.

title: Apportion by the Dean Method — app_dean
text: Dean method finds common divisor \(d\) rounds quotient harmonic mean two surrounding integers. quotient \(p_i / d\) falls integers \(n\) \(n + 1\) rounded \(n + 1\) exceeds harmonic mean: $$H(n,\, n+1) = \frac{2n(n+1)}{2n+1}$$ rounded \(n\) otherwise. divisor adjusted iteratively total allocation equals size. Among classical divisor methods, Dean method minimizes absolute difference average district population (.e., \(p_i / s_i\), \(s_i\) number seats awarded unit \(\)) two units. falls Webster Huntington-Hill methods treatment small versus large units, giving slightly seats small units Webster fewer Huntington-Hill.

title: Apportion by the Dean Method — app_dean

what: Apportion by the D'Hondt (Jefferson) Method — app_dhondt
title: Apportion by the D'Hondt (Jefferson) Method — app_dhondt
text: sequential priority method widely used proportional representation elections, including Belgium, Spain, Portugal, Netherlands, Austria, many countries. Mathematically equivalent Jefferson method, procedure described Thomas Jefferson. Compared Webster/Sainte-Laguë method, D'Hondt/Jefferson tends give slight advantage larger units.

title: Apportion by the D'Hondt (Jefferson) Method — app_dhondt
Apportion by the D'Hondt (Jefferson) Method — app_dhondt
text: size integer number seats apportion across units pop vector population sizes unit

title: Apportion by the D'Hondt (Jefferson) Method — app_dhondt
text: integer vector length pop number seats apportioned unit.

title: Apportion by the D'Hondt (Jefferson) Method — app_dhondt
text: D'Hondt method allocates seats sequentially. step, next seat awarded unit (party state) highest quotient: $$Q_i = \frac{p_i}{n_i + 1}$$ \(p_i\) population vote total unit \(\) \(n_i\) number seats currently holds. process repeats size seats awarded. Jefferson method finds common divisor \(d\) floor-rounded quotients sum desired house size: $$\sum_{} \left\lfloor \frac{p_i}{d} \right\rfloor = H$$ \(p_i\) population unit \(\) \(H\) total number seats (size). divisor \(d\) decreased iteratively condition met. Jefferson method mathematically equivalent D'Hondt method: produce allocation. Jefferson divisor formulation sequential D'Hondt priority \(p_i / (n_i + 1)\) two perspectives apportionment rule.

title: Apportion by the D'Hondt (Jefferson) Method — app_dhondt

what: Apportion by the Hamilton-Vinton Method — app_hamilton_vinton
title: Apportion by the Hamilton-Vinton Method — app_hamilton_vinton
text: largest-remainder quota method used US Congressional apportionment 1850 1900. Also known Hamilton method Vinton method.

title: Apportion by the Hamilton-Vinton Method — app_hamilton_vinton
Apportion by the Hamilton-Vinton Method — app_hamilton_vinton
text: size integer number seats apportion across units pop vector population sizes unit

title: Apportion by the Hamilton-Vinton Method — app_hamilton_vinton
text: integer vector length pop number seats apportioned unit.

title: Apportion by the Hamilton-Vinton Method — app_hamilton_vinton
text: Hamilton-Vinton method first computes exact quota unit: $$q_i = \frac{p_i \cdot H}{P}$$ \(p_i\) population unit \(\), \(H\) total number seats (size), \(P = \sum_i p_i\) total population. unit initially receives \(\lfloor q_i \rfloor\) seats. remaining seats awarded one time units largest fractional remainders \(q_i - \lfloor q_i \rfloor\). method satisfies quota property: unit ever receives fewer \(\lfloor q_i \rfloor\) \(\lceil q_i \rceil\) seats. However, susceptible "Alabama paradox," increasing total house size can paradoxically cause unit lose seat.

title: Apportion by the Hamilton-Vinton Method — app_hamilton_vinton

what: Apportion by the Huntington-Hill (Equal proportions) Method — app_huntington_hill
title: Apportion by the Huntington-Hill (Equal proportions) Method — app_huntington_hill
text: current method used US Congressional apportionment, continuous use since Apportionment Act 1941. Also known method equal proportions.

title: Apportion by the Huntington-Hill (Equal proportions) Method — app_huntington_hill
Apportion by the Huntington-Hill (Equal proportions) Method — app_huntington_hill
text: size integer number seats apportion across units pop vector population sizes unit

title: Apportion by the Huntington-Hill (Equal proportions) Method — app_huntington_hill
text: integer vector length pop number seats apportioned unit.

title: Apportion by the Huntington-Hill (Equal proportions) Method — app_huntington_hill
text: Huntington-Hill method sequential priority method. Starting one seat allocated unit (constitutional minimum Congressional apportionment), repeatedly awards next seat unit highest priority value: $$P_i(n) = \frac{p_i}{\sqrt{n(n + 1)}}$$ \(p_i\) population unit \(\) \(n\) number seats currently held unit \(\). equivalent divisor method rounds quotient geometric mean two surrounding integers: \(p_i / d\) rounded \(n + 1\) exceeds \(\sqrt{n(n+1)}\), \(n\) otherwise. Among divisor methods, Huntington-Hill minimizes maximum relative difference representation two units.

title: Apportion by the Huntington-Hill (Equal proportions) Method — app_huntington_hill
text: Huntington, E. V. (1928). apportionment representatives Congress. Transactions American Mathematical Society, 30(1), 85–110. doi:10.2307/1989268

title: Apportion by the Huntington-Hill (Equal proportions) Method — app_huntington_hill

what: Apportion by the Webster (Sainte-Laguë) Method — app_webster
title: Apportion by the Webster (Sainte-Laguë) Method — app_webster
text: divisor method uses standard arithmetic-mean rounding, used US Congressional apportionment 1842 1911 1931. Also used Norway Sweden parliamentary seat allocation (known Sainte-Laguë method).

title: Apportion by the Webster (Sainte-Laguë) Method — app_webster
Apportion by the Webster (Sainte-Laguë) Method — app_webster
text: size integer number seats apportion across units pop vector population sizes unit

title: Apportion by the Webster (Sainte-Laguë) Method — app_webster
text: integer vector length pop number seats apportioned unit.

title: Apportion by the Webster (Sainte-Laguë) Method — app_webster
text: Webster method finds common divisor \(d\) standard-rounded quotients sum desired house size: $$\sum_{} \text{round}\!\left(\frac{p_i}{d}\right) = H$$ \(p_i\) population unit \(\) \(H\) total number seats (size). Quotients rounded arithmetic mean \(n + 0.5\) consecutive integers \(n\) \(n + 1\). Among classical divisor methods, Webster considered statistically unbiased: systematically favor either large small units. minimizes expected absolute deviation exact quotas populations drawn wide range sizes.

title: Apportion by the Webster (Sainte-Laguë) Method — app_webster
apportion: Apportion Seats — apportion-package
text: Convert populations integer number seats legislative bodies. Implements apportionment methods used historically currently United States reapportionment Census, described https://www.census.gov/history/www/reference/apportionment/methods_of_apportionment.html.
